title: The Pillay Family Trail: The Old Ship & the Pilgrims' Crossing
area: River Wey towpath, Millmead to St Catherine's, Guildford
blurb: A short, flat early-morning riverside walk: follow the River Wey south out of town to a roofless pilgrims' chapel on the hill, a lost ferry crossing, and a glimpse of the 470-year-old Ship pub. Isaac cracks the clues, Eli spots the boats and ducks. ~2.2 km there-and-back, ~1 hr — with an optional longer leg to St Catherine's Lock if everyone's game.
difficulty: family · ages 2 & 6 · flat riverside towpath · buggy-friendly along the river (chapel climb is steps/sand — view from below or bail) · short core ~1 hr · optional lock leg adds ~30 min

start.name: Millbrook car park, central Guildford (towpath south)
start.directions: 8:30am start — towpath's quiet and Millbrook (GU1 3XJ, riverside, ~5 min from GU2) is easy to park. From the car park join the River Wey towpath and head SOUTH (Godalming direction), water on your right. SHORT CORE: town → St Catherine's chapel view → old-ferry footbridge (turnaround) → back = ~2.2 km / ~1 hr. Flat, buggy-friendly along the towpath. ⚠️ The Ship pub is shut this early (opens ~12) — it's a look-at-it stop, not lunch; reward = ice cream/picnic later, or come back another day for the pub. Chapel climb is steep sand + steps (no buggy) — optional, view it from below. Hold little hands near water/lock/bridge edges. Optional extension: carry on south to St Catherine's Lock (+~30 min) if everyone's game. 🍼 BUGGY over carrier: the towpath is flat and buggy-friendly, and on a hot day the buggy gives Eli shade + a nap spot and carries the water/picnic/spare-socks — a carrier just overheats him. Only the (skippable) chapel climb needs a sling; toss one in the basket as backup if you fancy going up. ☀️ HOT-DAY KIT: sun hats, suncream, lots of water, buggy shade/parasol, spare socks for Eli — and you're done before the midday heat.

stops.name: The Towpath South
stops.clue: Stand by the river where boats are lifted up and down through big wooden gates — a LOCK. Long ago, horses walked this very path pulling heavy barges by rope. Check in, then follow the water south like a barge horse! Which way is the river flowing?
stops.little_ones: Eli — wave at the water and find a duck or a boat to follow! 🦆
stops.grownup: You're on the Wey Navigation, opened in 1653 — one of Britain's first artificial waterways. Horses once towed barges along this towpath all the way to the Thames. Head south and the path skirts the base of St Catherine's Hill.
stops.story: This is the old towpath. Before engines, horses plodded along here pulling boats full of timber, corn and gunpowder up and down the river.

stops.name: The Pilgrims' Chapel on the Hill
stops.clue: Look UP to the sandy hill above the river. An old stone CHAPEL stands there — but something is missing from the top of it. Look carefully: does the chapel still have its ROOF, yes or no?
stops.answer_hint: You can see the sky right through the top of it now…
stops.little_ones: Eli — can you spot the castle-shaped building up on the hill? Point at it! 🏰
stops.grownup: St Catherine's Chapel was built in the early 1300s by Richard de Wauncey, rector of St Nicholas. King Edward II licensed a five-day fair on the hill in 1308. It's now a roofless sandstone ruin and a landmark on the old Pilgrims' Way. The climb is steep sand + steps — optional; admire it from the towpath if buggy-bound.
stops.story: Hundreds of years ago, pilgrims walking the Pilgrims' Way stopped to pray at this little chapel on the hill. Its roof is long gone, but the strong stone walls still stand.

stops.name: The Lost Ferry
stops.clue: Right below the chapel hill, you cross the river on a wooden FOOTBRIDGE. But long ago there was no bridge here — a person rowed people across in a little boat instead. What do we call someone who rows you across a river? (Starts with 'F'…)
stops.answer_hint: Same word as 'Ferry Lane' just up the path…
stops.little_ones: Eli — stand on the bridge and look down at the water rushing under your feet! 🌉
stops.grownup: This footbridge replaced an ancient foot-ferry that carried pilgrims and travellers across the Wey at the foot of St Catherine's Hill — hence Ferry Lane, the path up to the Ship. The ferry ran within living memory.
stops.story: For centuries there was no bridge here. A ferryman rowed pilgrims across the river at the bottom of the chapel hill. The bridge you're standing on took the ferry's job.

stops.name: St Catherine's Lock
stops.clue: OPTIONAL EXTRA (only if everyone's still going strong): keep south to a quiet LOCK in the open fields — the calmest, shallowest one on the whole river. Find its name sign. What is this lock called? (Clue: same saint as the chapel on the hill!)
stops.answer_hint: Same name as the chapel and the hill behind you…
stops.little_ones: Eli — watch the water spill through the gates. Can you hear it whooshing? 💦
stops.grownup: St Catherine's Lock is the shallowest on the Wey Navigation and over 250 years old, still working today, in an isolated spot surrounded by open fields toward Shalford. This is a good turnaround point — head back to the footbridge and up Ferry Lane for the Ship.
stops.story: St Catherine's Lock is the gentlest, shallowest lock on the river — and one of the oldest, working for over 250 years. This is our turnaround: now back to the bridge and up to the Ship!

stops.name: Ye Olde Ship Inn
stops.clue: Back over the bridge and up Ferry Lane is a very, VERY old pub — the Ship. People have eaten and drunk here for over 400 years! Find the year on the sign. Which is older: this pub, or your house? (And what year is on the Ship?)
stops.answer_hint: It begins with 15… the pub has been here since fifteen-hundred-and-something!
stops.little_ones: Eli — find the picture of the SHIP on the pub sign. Time for a treat! 🍦
stops.grownup: Ye Olde Ship Inn (St Catherine's Village, Portsmouth Road, GU2 4EB) dates to 1556 — over 400 years of trade, nestled between the River Wey and the North Downs Way. Dog- and family-friendly; the natural lunch reward. ~20 min back to town via Porridge Pot Alley, or retrace the towpath.
stops.story: Ye Olde Ship Inn has been serving travellers and pilgrims since 1556 — older than almost any building you know. The perfect end to a river pilgrimage: a treat at the Ship!

finale.title: River Pilgrims Isaac & Eli — you made the crossing! ⚓🎉
finale.message: You followed the old towpath south, found the roofless pilgrims' chapel, crossed where the lost ferry once rowed, and spotted the 470-year-old Ship. True River Pilgrims — show Mum and Dad all your stickers!
finale.reward_idea: An ice cream or picnic on the way home (the Ship's not open this early) — or come back another day for lunch at the pub. 🍦

credits: Map data © OpenStreetMap contributors · facts © National Trust / Exploring Surrey's Past / National Trails / Ye Olde Ship Inn
_recce_notes: PREP 2026-06-27 from Dan's request ('river nature trail by the Ship'). SOUTHERN Wey towpath — complements the existing northern/central riverside trails (Burpham Riverside, Dapdune/Wey Barge); shared anchor = Millmead, but chapel/ferry/St Catherine's Lock/Ship are all new. Route: central Guildford (Millmead/Town Wharf) → towpath SOUTH → St Catherine's Hill + chapel (optional steep climb) → old-ferry footbridge → St Catherine's Lock (turnaround) → back over bridge + up Ferry Lane to Ye Olde Ship Inn (lunch). ~3.5 km there-and-back, flexes 1.5–2.5 hr; bail at the footbridge to skip the lock. Flat, buggy-friendly ALONG THE TOWPATH; chapel climb is steps + loose sand (NO buggy — view from below). Water/lock edges throughout — hold little hands. ALL PINS ARE SOFT, FOOT-VERIFY + retune geofences: Millmead start (1), St Catherine's Chapel (2 — hilltop ~51.2293,-0.5793), ferry footbridge (3), St Catherine's Lock (4 — ~51.2247,-0.5749, isolated fields, may need to walk further than the pin), Ye Olde Ship Inn (5 — Portsmouth Rd GU2 4EB; pin ~51.2312,-0.5790, confirm exact frontage + the '1556' sign). Durable clues: roofless chapel, lock name sign, Ship '1556'. Parking: town car parks (pub car park is for patrons). Older-kid extension: climb St Catherine's Hill for the view + the chapel up close.
